coredata.dc:title: Assay of inorganic pyrophosphatase activity based on a fluorescence “turn–off” strategy using carbon quantum dots@Cu-MOF nanotubes
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Ningning
Zhao, Lixin
He, Mengting
Luo, Peng
Tan, Liang
coredata.dc:description: A highly sensitive and selective sensor for the quantitative assay of inorganic pyrophosphatase (PPase) activity was developed based on a fluorescence “turn–off” strategy. Carbon quantum dots@Cu(II)-based metal–organic framework nanotubes (CQDs@Cu-MOF) with length less than 300 nm and width less than 20 nm were synthesized. CQDs in the nanotubes exhibited weak fluorescence owing to static quenching. The coordination reaction between pyrophosphate ion (PPi) and Cu(II) decomposed CQDs@Cu-MOF and led to the release of CQDs, of which the fluorescence recovered. In the presence of PPase, the hydrolysis of PPi generated phosphate ion (Pi). CQDs@Cu-MOF remained their structural stability and the fluorescence turned off. The fluorescence intensity difference of the mixture of CQDs@Cu-MOF and PPi in the absence and presence of PPase (-ΔF) was proportional to the PPase concentration from 0.1 to 5 mU mL−1 and that from 5 to 50 mU mL−1, and a limit of detection at 0.03 mU mL−1 was obtained. PPase activity in human serum was analyzed using the proposed fluorescence sensor and the recovery values were found to vary from 95.0% to 104 %.
coredata.dcterms:subject: carbon quantum dots@Cu-MOF nanotubes
Pyrophosphate ion
Coordination reaction
Inorganic pyrophosphatase
Hydrolysis
Fluorescence “turn–off”

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Mechanism of CQDs@Cu-MOF fluorescence change in the presence of PPi and PPase

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Hunan Provincial Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Natural Science Foundation of Hunan Province

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by Changsha Municipal Natural Science Foundation ( kq2014074 ) and Hunan Provincial Natural Science Foundation of China ( 2021JJ30439 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image of CQDs, inset: particle size distribution. (B) Fluorescence excitation (a) and emission spectra (b) of CQDs. (C) FTIR spectrum of CQDs.
(A-B) TEM images of CQDs@Cu-MOF. (C-D) SEM backscattered electron image (C) and EDS elemental distribution maps (D) of CQDs@Cu-MOF.

(A) Fluorescence emission spectra of 0.67 mg mL-1 CQDs@Cu-MOF in pH 7.4 Tris-HCl in the absence (a) and presence of 30 mU mL-1 PPase (b), 70 μM PPi (c), and the 10 mU mL-1 PPase-70 μM PPi mixture (d). Excitation slit: 5 nm, emission slit: 5 nm. (B) UV–vis absorption spectra of CQDs (a), 0.67 mg mL-1 CQDs@Cu-MOF (b), the 0.67 mg mL-1 CQDs@Cu-MOF-70 μM PPi mixture (c), and the mixture consisting of 0.67 mg mL-1 CQDs@Cu-MOF, 70 μM PPi and 10 mU mL-1 PPase (d).
Schematic illustration of the fluorescence changes due to the relevant reactions.

(A) Fluorescence emission spectra of 0.67 mg mL-1 CQDs@Cu-MOF in pH 7.4 Tris-HCl containing PPi with different concentrations. The PPi concentration (from a to o): 0, 0.5, 1, 3, 5, 10, 20, 30, 40, 50, 60, 70, 80 and 90 μM. Excitation slit: 5 nm, emission slit: 5 nm. (B) Plots of the fluorescence intensity vs. the concentration of PPi. (C-D) The effects of pH (C) and the reaction time (D) on the difference between the fluorescence intensity of 0.67 mg mL-1 GQDs@Cu-MOF in pH 7.4 Tris-HCl containing 70 μM PPi and that in pH 7.4 Tris-HCl containing the 70 μM PPi-30 mU mL- 1 PPase mixture (ΔF 1). Results are presented as mean ± SD (error bar) of triplicate experiments.

(A) Fluorescence emission spectra of 0.67 mg mL-1 CQDs@Cu-MOF in pH 7.4 Tris-HCl contaning 70 μM PPi and PPase with different concentrations. The concentration of PPase (from a to k): 0, 0.1, 0.5, 1, 3, 5, 10, 20, 30, 40, 50 mU mL-1. (B) Plots of the fluorescence intensity difference (ΔF 2) vs. the concentration of PPase. Results are presented as mean SD (error bar) of triplicate experiments.

The fluorescence intensity changes of 0.67 mg mL-1 CQDs@Cu-MOF in pH 7.4 Tris-HCl containing 70 μM PPi before and after the addition of some biomolecules (1–8), 30 mU mL-1 PPase (9), and the mixture consisting of 30 mU mL-1 PPase and some biomolecules (10–17) (ΔF 2). The concentration of biomolecules: 30 mU mL-1 GOx (1,10), 30 mU mL-1 HRP (2,11), 30 mU mL-1 typsin (3,12), 40 mg mL-1 HSA (4,13), 10 mM glucose (5,14), 0.4 mM UA (6,15), 30 μM Trp (7,16),and 30 μM Cys (8,17).
Comparison of fluorescent probes, linear ranges and LOD values of some PPase fluorescence senosrs.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: NCs: nanoclusters, 11-MUA: 11-mercaptoundecanoic acid, GQDs: graphene quantum dots, Glo: globulin, FPNPs: fluorescence polymer nanoparticles.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Determination of PPase activity in human serum samples (n = 3).

head.ce:title: Assay of inorganic pyrophosphatase activity based on a fluorescence “turn–off” strategy using carbon quantum dots@Cu-MOF nanotubes

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Chemical Biology and Traditional Chinese Medicine Research (Ministry of Education of China), College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Hunan Normal University, Changsha, 410081, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Chemical Biology and Traditional Chinese Medicine Research (Ministry of Education of China), College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Hunan Normal University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Chemical Biology and Traditional Chinese Medicine Research (Ministry of Education of China), College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Hunan Normal University, Changsha, 410081, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A highly sensitive and selective sensor for the quantitative assay of inorganic pyrophosphatase (PPase) activity was developed based on a fluorescence “turn–off” strategy. Carbon quantum dots@Cu(II)-based metal–organic framework nanotubes (CQDs@Cu-MOF) with length less than 300 nm and width less than 20 nm were synthesized. CQDs in the nanotubes exhibited weak fluorescence owing to static quenching. The coordination reaction between pyrophosphate ion (PPi) and Cu(II) decomposed CQDs@Cu-MOF and led to the release of CQDs, of which the fluorescence recovered. In the presence of PPase, the hydrolysis of PPi generated phosphate ion (Pi). CQDs@Cu-MOF remained their structural stability and the fluorescence turned off. The fluorescence intensity difference of the mixture of CQDs@Cu-MOF and PPi in the absence and presence of PPase (-ΔF) was proportional to the PPase concentration from 0.1 to 5 mU mL−1 and that from 5 to 50 mU mL−1, and a limit of detection at 0.03 mU mL−1 was obtained. PPase activity in human serum was analyzed using the proposed fluorescence sensor and the recovery values were found to vary from 95.0% to 104 %.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Inorganic pyrophosphatase (PPase) is a water-soluble hydrolase that has been found in living organisms. This enzyme is highly active in the presence of magnesium ions (Mg2+) or transition metal ions, such as manganese (Mn2+) and cobalt (Co2+), and it can irreversibly hydrolyze one pyrophosphate ion (PPi, P2O7 4-) into two phosphates ions (Pi, PO4 3-) [1]. PPase participates in the energy cycle during the hydrolysis process of PPi and releases a large amount of energy, which can provide power for biosynthesis reactions [2]. Investigations have revealed that PPase plays an important role in lipid metabolism including lipid synthesis and degradation, bone formation, DNA synthesis, calcium absorption and other biological processes [3–5]. PPase expression is also related to clinical diseases including gastric cancer, ovarian cancer, breast cancer [6–8]. At present, some methods have been developed for the detection of PPase, such as photocurrent analysis [9], colorimetry [10], electrochemiluminescence [11] and fluorescence [12]. Developing a simple and efficient technique for the highly sensitive assay of PPase, which is valuable for disease diagnosis and prophylaxis, is still a goal pursued by researchers.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), also known as carbon dots (CDs), are zero-dimensional (0 D) spherical nanoparticles with a size less than 10 nm [13]. CQDs have excellent properties, for example, abundant precursor sources, stable luminescence, easy surface functionalization and modification, low toxicity, good biocompatibility, and so on, which have attracted extensive attention of researchers [14]. Currently, CQDs have been widely used in nanomedicine [15], photocatalysis [16], biosensing [17] and bioimaging [18]. Due to some extraordinary optical properties, CQDs have found their application as an efficient fluorescent nanoprobe in the detection of ions [19], drug molecules [20] and biomolecules [21], etc.

Metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) are porous framework crystalline materials, which are composed of inorganic ions and organic building blocks, and can be prepared via a molecular-level self-assembly process [22]. MOFs exhibit many advantages, such as large surface area, high pore capacity, good adsorption ability, excellent catalytic performance, high chemical stability and unique morphology, which make them widely employed in gas separation [23], selective adsorption [24], catalysis [25], sensing [26], energy storage [27], drug transport system [28], and so on. In recent years, it has been reported that fluorescent substances, enzymes and drugs were encapsulated in MOFs to produce new functionalized nanomaterials. For example, core–shell nanocomposites composed of CdTe QDs and zeolitic imidazolium framework (ZIF-8) was prepared and they displayed excellent water dispersibility and high stability [29]. A research reveals that CQDs played a synergistic role in enhancing the activity and thermal/storage stability of Candida rugosa lipase encapsulated in ZIF-8 [30]. A novel magnetic metal–organic framework Fe3O4@PAA@ZIF-8 was used to encapsulate antibiotic ciprofloxacin (CIP), which has the ability to transport and release drugs under human physiological conditions [31].

In this work, CQDs were prepared via hydrothermal method using o-phenylenediamine and citric acid as precursors. Carbon quantum dots@Cu-MOF nanotubes (CQDs@Cu-MOF) were synthesized using CQDs, copper ions and 2-methylimidazole. The nanocomposites were characterized by field emission scanning electron microscopy and energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (FE-SEM/EDS), transmission electron microscopy (TEM), UV–vis absorption spectroscopy (UV–vis) and Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR). The fluorescence intensity of the CQDs@Cu-MOF suspension in the absence and presence of PPi, PPase and their mixture was measured, respectively. The experimental conditions for the assay of PPase activity including the concentration of PPi, pH and the reaction time were optimized. The selectivity of the sensor was assessed, and PPase activity in human serum was determined by standard addition method.

Citric acid (CA), copper chloride (CuCl2·2H2O), 2-methylimidazole (2-MI), tris(hydroxymethyl)aminomethane (Tris), hydrochloric acid (HCl), o-phenylenediamine (o-PD), sodium pyrophosphate (Na4P2O7·10H2O), magnesium chloride (MgCl2) and sodium hydroxide (NaOH) were all products of Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (China). PPase was purchased from Merck & Co Inc (USA). Glucose oxidase (GOx), horseradish peroxidase (HRP), trypsin, human serum albumin (HSA), glucose, uric acid (UA), tryptophane (Trp) and cysteine (Cys) were purchased from Aladdin Industrial Corporation (China). Human serum was obtained from the Hospital of Hunan Normal University. Tris-HCl buffer solution that was prepared by mixing 20 mM Tris and 0.1 M HCl was used to control pH. The solutions used in the experiments were prepared with Milli-Q ultrapure water (Millipore, 18.25 MΩ).

The morphology characterization and elemental analysis of CQDs@Cu-MOF were performed using a FEI Nova NanoSEM 230 FE-SEM (USA). The morphology and size of CQDs and CQDs@Cu-MOF were observed on a FEI Tecnai G2 20ST TEM (USA). The absorption spectra were recorded with a Shimadzu UV-2450 UV–vis spectrophotometer (Japan). Infrared spectroscopy was recorded with a Thermo Nicolet Nexus 670 FTIR R spectrometer (Japan). The fluorescence spectra were collected with a Hitachi F-7000 fluorophotometer (Japan).

CQDs were prepared according to the reported hydrothermal method [32]. Firstly, 0.63 g o-PD and 1.38 g CA were dispersed in 30 mL of ultrapure water and then the mixture was sonicated for 10 min. Next, the solution was kept in a Telfon-lined stainless steel autoclave at 200 °C for 5 h. After cooling to room temperature, the suspension was centrifuged at 12,000 rpm for 30 min. The supernatant was collected and it was dialyzed using a 1000 D dialysis bag for 24 h. The prepared CQDs suspension was stored in a refrigerator at 4 °C for further use.

CQDs@Cu-MOF were synthetized based on the reported method preparing disulfiram-loaded Cu-MOF [33]. 0.0083 g CuCl2·2H2O was dissolved in 4.3 mL of H2O (pH 9, adjusted by NaOH), and then 2 mL of 2 mg mL−1 CQDs suspension was added under continuous stirring. Then, 10 mL of H2O (pH 9) containing 0.1845 g 2-MI was introduced and the mixture was stirred for 15 min. The supernatant was separated via centrifugation at 12,000 rpm for 15 min. The precipitate was washed with ultrapure water for three times and it was dried in a hot air oven at 60 °C.

10 µL of PPase with different concentration was added into 150 µL of pH 7.4 Tris-HCl buffer solution containing 70 µM PPi and 20 mM Mg2+. The reaction lasted for 30 min at 37 °C. The solution was mixed with 20 µL of 10 mg mL−1 CQDs@Cu-MOF suspension, and then ultrapure water was introduced until the liquid volume arrived at 300 μL. Finally, the mixture was shaken thoroughly. The fluorescence emission spectra were obtained in the wavelength range of 394–600 nm at the excitation wavelength of 374 nm. The slit widths for both the excitation and emission monochromators were fixed at 5.0 nm.

The TEM image, fluorescence spectra and FTIR spectrum of CQDs are shown in Fig. 1 . As shown in Fig. 1A, CQDs were in a well-dispersed state. Their size was in the range of 0.7–2.5 nm and the average diameter was 1.6 nm. Fig. 1B shows that the fluorescence excitation peak and emission peak of CQDs were located at 374 nm and 445 nm, respectively, and Stokes shift was more than 70 nm. Fig. 1C exhibits some characteristic absorption bands in the infrared spectroscopy of CQDs. The strong absorption peak at 3444 cm−1 was derived from the stretching vibration of OH. The absorption peak at 1631 cm−1 was associated with the stretching vibration of CO. The absorption peak at 1085 cm−1 was ascribed to the CO stretching vibration. The above characteristic absorption bands confirmed the existence of COOH group in the as-prepared CQDs. A weak absorption peak at 1386 cm−1 was assigned to the C-N bending vibration that suggests the existence of amines on CQDs since o-phenylenediamine was used as a precursor for the preparation of this carbon-based quantum dots.

Fig. 2 shows the TEM image, SEM image, and elemental mapping of CQDs@Cu-MOF. One can observe from Fig. 2A that the prepared materials were hollow nanotubes with length less than 300 nm and width less than 20 nm, the morphology of which has been reported [34]. As indicated by the arrows in the TEM image that aimed at the selected area in Fig. 2A, some nanodots were present in porous nanotubes (Fig. 2B). Fig. 2C and 2D display the SEM backscattered electron image and EDS elemental distribution maps, respectively. The former reveals that CQDs@Cu-MOF with uniform morphology were evenly distributed on the substrate. The latter demonstrates the existence of Cu, C, N and O elements. Copper was the key component element in Cu-MOF. Carbon and nitrogen could be found in CQDs and 2-MI. O element might originate from COOH on CQDs. It is reported that the reaction between 2-MI and copper(II) chloride could generate the complexs, (MIz)2CuCl2 and (MIz)4CuCl2, in which MIz represented 2-methylimidazole. The former has a tetrahedral structure and the latter owns a tetragonally distorted octahedral structure [35]. The combination of Cu(II) with 2-MI is achieved via Cu-N bond [36]. It is a key basis for the formation of the Cu-based metal–organic framework. The TEM and SEM measurement results suggest that these hollow and porous nanotubes were the product of the following reaction.
(1)
CQDs + C u ( I I ) + 2 - M I → C Q D s @ C u - M O F

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of CQDs@Cu-MOF fluorescence change in the presence of PPi and PPase
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence emission spectra of the CQDs@Cu-MOF suspension in the absence and presence of PPi, PPase and the PPi-PPase mixture were measured, respectively, and the results are shown in Fig. 3 A. The fluorescence intensity of CQDs@Cu-MOF was quite low (curve a), indicating the effective fluorescence quenching of CQDs in the nanotubes. After the addition of PPase, the fluorescence intensity of CQDs@Cu-MOF hardly changed (curve b), meaning that PPase had no effect on the stability of CQDs@Cu-MOF. However, the mixing solution of CQDs@Cu-MOF and PPi exhibited significantly enhanced fluorescence at 445 nm, i.e., the fluorescence of CQDs recovered (curve c). It suggests that free CQDs were released from CQDs@Cu-MOF after PPi was introduced into the mixture. When PPi and PPase co-existed, the fluorescence intensity of CQDs@Cu-MOF was found to turn off (curve d), which reveals that the hydrolysis product of PPi after incubation with PPase, orthophosphate, contributed little to the release of CQDs.

The UV absorption spectra of CQDs, CQDs@Cu-MOF, the mixing solution of CQDs@Cu-MOF and PPi, and the mixture consisting of CQDs@Cu-MOF, PPi and PPase were investigated, respectively, in order to demonstrate the mechanism of the above fluorescence change. As shown in Fig. 3B, CQDs had characteristic doublet absorption peaks at 268 and 274 nm (curve a), which was attributed to π–π* transition of CC bond. On the absorption spectrum of CQDs@Cu-MOF, these two peaks were absent but a low and broad peak at about 320 nm appeared (curve b). It reveals the absence of free CQDs and the presence of a new product that was confirmed by Fig. 2. Some carboxyl-/amino-functionalized CDs have been prepared and these functional groups on CDs had strong ability to combine selectively with copper ions [37,38]. The fluorescence of CDs could be quenched by some metal ions, such as Cu2+ [39], and Fe2+/Fe3+ [40], through the static quenching mechanism. Hence, it is deduced from the UV–vis absorption and fluorescence emission spectra of CQDs@Cu-MOF that CQDs constituted in the new nanocomposites owing to the binding of both carboxyl groups and amino groups on CQDs with Cu(II). The low fluorescence of CQDs@Cu-MOF was the result of static quenching. The absorption peak at 320 nm disappeared and the characteristic peaks of CQDs re-emerged on the UV–vis spectrum of the mixing solution containing CQDs@Cu-MOF and PPi (curve c). It is known that the reaction between PPi and Cu(II) could produce Cu(P2O7)2 6- with a high stability constant (K) of 1012.45 [41]. Curve c in Fig. 3A and 3B reveals the following reaction due to the higher binding affinity between PPi and Cu(II) than that between CQDs (or 2-MI) with Cu(II).
CQDs @ C u - M O F + 2 P P i → C u
PPi
2
+ C Q D s + 2 - M I

As a result, CQDs@Cu-MOF suffered from disintegration and the fluorescence probe, CQDs, were released. The relevant process is shown by Path I in Fig. 4 . After PPase and PPi were added into the CQDs@Cu-MOF suspension, the two characteristic absorption peaks of CQDs became weak once more (curve d). It indicates that PPase catalyzed the hydrolysis of PPi to Pi and the latter had a low affinity to Cu(II). As a result, CQDs@Cu-MOF remained their structural stability and a few free CQDs existed in the mixture. Path II in Fig. 4 shows the relevant procedure.

The above experimental results reveal the feasibility of detection of PPase activity. To improve the detection sensitivity, some condition optimization experiments were carried out. The effect of the PPi content on the fluorescence of 0.67 mg mL−1 CQDs@Cu-MOF was investigated and the results are displayed in Fig. 5 A. The fluorescence intensity of the mixture was enhanced continuously with the increase of the PPi concentration, which means that CQDs@Cu-MOF was destroyed gradually and more and more CQDs were released from the nanotubes. Fig. 5B shows that the fluorescence intensity of CQDs@Cu-MOF in the presence of PPi, F, was linearly dependent on the concentration of PPi in the range of 0.5–70 μM. The linear regression equation was found to be F = 6.35c PPi + 146 (r = 0.998). When the PPi concentration was higher than 70 μM, the F value was almost unchanged. It indicates that almost all CQDs were set free from CQDs-Cu(II)-2-MI in this case. Therefore, 70 μM was selected as the optimal concentration of PPi in the following experiments to obtain the maximal amount of fluorescence probe in the solution.

Since PPase played a key role in the hydrolysis of PPi, two important factors affecting catalytic reaction, pH and the reaction time, were assessed. The fluorescence intensity of the mixture consisting of 0.67 mg mL−1 CQDs@Cu-MOF and 70 μM PPi in the absence (F a) and in the presence (F b) of 30 mU mL−1 PPase were measured, respectively, and the difference, ΔF 1 (ΔF 1 = F a – F b) was calculated. Fig. 5C and 5D shows the ΔF 1 value obtained at different pH and the reaction time. Fig. 5C indicates that the fluorescence intensity difference almost increased linearly when pH varied from 6.6 to 7.4 and the former suffered from decline with the further enhancement of the latter from 7.4 to 8.2. In acidic medium, the side reaction of PPi and H+ could inhibit the combination of PPi with Cu(II), which went against the decomposition of CQDs@Cu-MOF and the release of CQDs. Accordingly, the relatively lower F a value was obtained. Alkaline medium might have a negative influence on the catalytic activity of PPase, which resulted in the relatively higher F b value. So the ΔF 1 value became relatively smaller in these two cases. The optimal pH was selected at 7.4 since the largest ΔF 1 was obtained in this near-neutral medium. It is observed from Fig. 5D that the increase of the reaction time in the range of 15–30 min caused gradual improvement of the fluorescence intensity change and the ΔF 1 value remained constant after 0.5 h. The experimental results suggest that the adequate reaction time was necessary for the hydrolysis of PPi induced by PPase. So 30 min was chosen as the optimal reaction time in the measurement of PPase.

The fluorescence emission spectra of the mixture consisting 0.67 mg mL−1 CQDs@Cu-MOF, 70 µM PPi and PPase with different concentrations in pH 7.4 Tris-HCl were collected and the results are shown in Fig. 6 A. The fluorescence intensity of the mixture at 445 nm was found to decrease gradually when the concentration of PPase increased from 0.1 to 50 mU mL−1. One can conclude that the hydrolysis of PPi was accelerated with the enhancement of the enzyme. The larger PPase amount was, the more complete the hydrolysis extent was, and the less CQDs were released. Fig. 6B displays a positive correlation between the fluorescence intensity difference and the concentration of PPase from 0.1 to 5 mU mL−1 and that from 5 to 50 mU mL−1, respectively. The linear regression equations were ΔF 1 = 14.7c PPase + 15.7 (r = 0.991) and ΔF 1 = 4.59c PPase + 71.3 (r = 0.996). When the signal to noise ratio (S/N) was 3, the limit of detection (LOD) was calculated to be 0.03 mU mL−1. Some PPase fluorescence sensors are in Table 1 summarized, in which fluorescence probe, linear range and LOD are listed [42–48]. Compared with others, the proposed fluorescence sensor in this work owned a relatively wider linear range and lower LOD value.

Possible interference of some biomolecules including GOx, HRP, trypsin, HSA, glucose, UA, Trp and Cys for the PPase detection was investigated. The fluorescence intensity of the 0.67 mg mL−1 GQDs@Cu-MOF-70 µM PPi mixture in the absence (F a) and presence (F c) of the above biomolecules and the mixture consisting of 30 mU mL−1 PPase and these biomolecules were measured, respectively. The fluorescence intensity difference, ΔF 2 (ΔF 2 = F a – F c) are shown in Fig. 7 . It is found that the ΔF 2 value induced by the potential interferents was relatively smaller. Furthermore, the fluorescence intensity difference caused by the PPase-biomolecule mixture were near to that derived from PPase. The relative change of these ΔF 2 values were in the range of −8%-4%. It means that the selective detection of PPase could be realized in the presence of these biomolecules.

PPase activity in human serum samples was determined by standard addition method to evaluate the accuracy of the developed fluorescence sensor. PPase with different concentration was added human serum that was diluted with pH 7.4 Tris-HCl buffer solution and the measurement results are listed in Table 2 . The recovery of determination varied from 95% to 104% (RSD less than 6, n = 3), indicating that the fluorescence sensor had a high accuracy and it could be used for the quantitative assay of PPase activity in biological samples.

In this work, a fluorescence “turn–off” strategy was proposed for the detection of PPase activity. The strong fluorescence of CQDs was quenched once carbon quantum dots combined with Cu(II) and formed CQDs@Cu-MOF nanotubes. The decomposition of CQDs@Cu-MOF took place and CQDs were released due to the coordination reaction between PPi and Cu(II), which resulted in the fluorescence recovery. The PPase-catalyzed hydrolysis of PPi produced Pi and the latter had a little influence on the structural stability of CQDs@Cu-MOF that displayed the weak fluorescence. The quantitative assay of PPase activity was realized based on the fluorescence change. A wide detection range, low LOD, good selectivity and high accuracy were involved in the proposed sensor. It may provide a convenient approach for researches on disease diagnostics and drug discovery corresponding to PPase activity.

Ningning Zhang: Data curation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Lixin Zhao: Formal analysis, Investigation. Mengting He: Formal analysis, Investigation. Peng Luo: Formal analysis, Investigation. Liang Tan: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Investigation, Supervision, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by Changsha Municipal Natural Science Foundation (kq2014074) and Hunan Provincial Natural Science Foundation of China (2021JJ30439).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Clinical significance and functional validation of inorganic pyrophosphatase in diffuse large B cell lymphoma in humans

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L. Li, Aruna, D. Luo, A. Jin, Clinical significance and functional validation of inorganic pyrophosphatase in diffuse large B cell lymphoma in humans. Cytotechnology 70 (2018) 641–649.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A complete structural description of the catalytic cycle of yeast pyrophosphatase

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: E. Oksanen, A.K. Ahonen, H. Tuominen, V. Tuominen, R. Laht, A. Goldman, P. Heikinheimo, A complete structural description of the catalytic cycle of yeast pyrophosphatase. Biochemistry 46 (2007) 1228–1239.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Role of inorganic pyrophosphatase in the mechanism of action of parathyroid hormone and calcitonin

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H. Orimo, M. Ohata, T. Fujita, Role of inorganic pyrophosphatase in the mechanism of action of parathyroid hormone and calcitonin. Endocrinology 89 (1971) 852–858.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Inorganic pyrophosphatase induces type I collagen in osteoblasts

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M.D. Polewski, K.A. Johnson, M. Foster, J.L. Millan, R. Terkeltaub, Inorganic pyrophosphatase induces type I collagen in osteoblasts. Bone 46 (2010) 81–90.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: PYP-1, inorganic pyrophosphatase, is required for larval development and intestinal function in C. elegans

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: K.M. Ko, W. Lee, J.R. Yu, J. Ahnn, PYP-1, inorganic pyrophosphatase, is required for larval development and intestinal function in C. elegans. FEBS Lett. 581 (2007) 5445–5453.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Inorganic pyrophosphatase (PPA1) is a negative prognostic marker for human gastric cancer

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Yang, J. Cai, J. Yin, D. Wang, Z. Bai, J. Zhang, K. Wang, G. Yu, Z. Zhang, Inorganic pyrophosphatase (PPA1) is a negative prognostic marker for human gastric cancer. Int. J. Clin. Exp. Pathol. 8 (2015) 12482–12490.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Expression of inorganic pyrophosphatase (PPA1) correlates with poor prognosis of epithelial ovarian cancer

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Identification of critical elements for regulation of inorganic pyrophosphatase (PPA1) in MCF7 breast cancer cells
